title: Strategy-Proof Multi-Issue Mediation: An Application to Online Dispute Resolution
abstract: Mediation (assisted negotiation) is the preferred alternative dispute resolution approach that has given rise to a multibillion-dollar industry worldwide. Online dispute resolution (ODR) providers rely heavily on mechanized e-negotiation systems. Inspired by their structured negotiation systems, we follow a market design approach and develop a tractable framework in search of strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational mediation mechanisms. We characterize the full family of such mechanisms and the domains of preferences in which they exist. A necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of such protocols is the so-called quid pro quo property; a weak condition that formulates preferences for compromise solutions.

author.Onur Kesten: School of Economics, University of Sydney, Sydney, New South Wales 2050, Australia;
author.Selçuk Özyurt: Department of Economics, York University, Toronto, Ontario M3J 1P3, Canada

article.1. Introduction: The classic 1981 bestseller bookGetting to Yes, by Fisher and Ury, identified conflict as a growth industry, and the last few decades have proved them right. Judicial systems of developing and emerging economies are often challenged with a large backlog of cases, and efficiency concerns fuel the implementation of reforms that focus on increased usage of alternative dispute resolution (ADR) processes.1Mediation is often the preferred form of ADR due to its cost-effectiveness (time-wise and financially),2flexibility and confidentiality. Since the 1990s, a significant number of countries have implemented both mandatory and voluntary mediation programs to improve the efficacy of their legal systems. A structured and rigorous view of mediation is pioneered in online dispute resolution (ODR), which often relies on automation. ODR systems resolve disputes that arise both online and offline. In a standard ODR system, parties interact through an online platform, and the mediator is usually patented software, also known as an e-negotiation system (ENS), that follows predetermined sets of rules embodied in a mechanized algorithm. Over 140 ODR platforms currently operate worldwide,3while SmartSettle, SquareTrade, and Cybersettle are the oldest and probably the most prominent ones. It is estimated that e-commerce platforms like eBay, PayPal, Uber, and Amazon resolved more than a billion disputes in 2017 through their ODR systems (Habuka and Rule 2017). In the United States and Canada, more than 115 courts either partially or fully integrated ODR into their systems.4Rapid technological developments and worldwide changes brought by the COVID-19 pandemic have shown that ODR may arguably be the inevitable future of dispute resolution in the new millennium.5 In principle, ODR systems are ideal platforms to deliver impartial, consistent, and fair outcomes since the human factor (i.e., the mediator) is taken out of the equation and replaced with a set of reliable and objective rules and procedures. The task of automating a negotiation process is not a simple one, and this is evidenced by a myriad of systems (mostly still research efforts) around the world (Thiessen et al. 2012). However, existing systems are vulnerable to strategic manipulation, and negotiators face the daunting task of finding optimal strategies. This weakness is acknowledged by the experts in the field: A concern with the use of e-negotiation systems [ENS] is the possible effects of gaming and cheating. By supplying false information concerning the range of issues [i.e., alternatives] over which they are willing to negotiate, the results will be distorted.Thiessen et al. (1998) These distortions may cause severe inefficiencies: Negotiators may fail to achieve the best possible solution despite successfully reaching a resolution.Thiessen et al. (1998), the developers of the popular SmartSettle protocols, defend the current systems in this account as follows: It is not clear from various experiments carried out that these distortions will always be to the benefit of the cheater. It may turn out, however, that if everyone cheats, the alternatives ENS generates may be negotiable and therefore useful in the negotiation process even though they [i.e., outcomes] may not be truly …efficient. While pioneers in the field remain optimistic, a platform that is prone to gaming may produce systematically unfair and inefficient outcomes or may even declare an impasse for an otherwise resolvable dispute. In fact,Bolton et al. (2018)show that poorly designed dispute resolution systems in online markets, which allow strategic gaming of feedback withdrawal options, create more escalation rather than resolution. Infrequent users (e.g., customers in e-commerce disputes, individual plaintiffs against companies, and government agencies) may be disadvantaged when they face experienced users who have accumulated enough expertise about how to game the system. Although designingfairandefficiente-negotiation systems is an active research interest in a highly interdisciplinary domain, existing models do not take incentive considerations into account. Inspired by the structured mediation programs that are offered by the ODR systems, we follow a mechanism design approach to develop a tractable framework in search of efficient, incentive-compatible, and impartial mediation mechanisms. Mechanism design has been successful in many applications, most notably in market design for auctions and matching. Much of the traditional mechanism design approach to bargaining, pioneered by the seminal work ofMyerson and Satterthwaite (1983), equips negotiators with quasi-linear utility. Although money is an important issue in disputes, it is rarely the only issue (Malhotra and Bazerman 2008). This underscores the necessity of a setting that can also admit nontransferable utility specifications and nonmonetary issues. For this reason, we adopt anordinalapproach whereby negotiators reveal only their bargaining ranges (i.e., range of negotiable alternatives) rather than their full-fledged (cardinal) preferences. There are three advantages of this approach. First, rather than restricting players’ preferences to a specific transferable utility setting, we maintain a basic common implication of any monotonic preferences in a conflict situation. Doing so allows us to characterize all classes of preferences that would support a possibility result and thereby support both transferable and nontransferable utility frameworks. Second, it is genuinely simple to implement ordinal mechanisms in practice, which is particularly important when agents are boundedly rational.6Third, the ordinal approach together with dominant strategy implementation (i.e., strategy-proofness) makes it possible to avoid the famous critique ofWilson (1987)by providing “detail-freeness” and “robust incentives” to participants. While stressing the powerful insights that mechanism design offers in bargaining problems,Ausubel et al. (2002)voice a similar concern: Despite these virtues, mechanism design has two weaknesses. First, the mechanisms depend in complex ways on the traders’ beliefs and utility functions, which are assumed to be common knowledge. …In practice, bargainers use simple trading rules—such as a sequence of offers and counteroffers—that do not depend on beliefs or utility functions. Despite the abundance of well-known strategy-proof mechanisms in a number of other contexts, we are not aware of any prior work that studies strategy-proof negotiation mechanisms. (SeeSection 6for a detailed discussion of the related literature.) E-negotiation protocols that are used by ODR platforms usually take issues and possible alternatives in every single issue as given. This information is often solicited when parties describe the dispute at the time they first request the service of the ODR platform. The negotiation problem is then created by populating this information on the system. Aside from this preliminary step, protocols generally involve three common steps: (1)elicitation, (2)proposal, and (3)ratification. The process ends either when parties unanimously accept a proposal or if no mutual agreement is reached after several iterations of an “elicitation-proposal-ratification” cycle. The goal of the elicitation step is to obtain the parties’ private information. First, parties are asked about their bargaining ranges for each issue, with the understanding that the mediator’s proposal will never include an alternative outside this range. Given the negotiators’ positions (i.e., how negotiators rank the alternatives), the system infers the set of negotiable alternatives once they declare their bargaining ranges.7Protocols usually differ in how they process all this information to make recommendations, and the majority of the existing e-negotiation protocols use a combination of cooperative game-theoretic and optimization techniques. The backbone of our formal setting consists of two negotiators who are in a dispute over two issues, a main and a supplementary issue. According to negotiation experts, success in mediation lies in the parties’ ability to expand the pie and find integrative (win-win) outcomes, which necessitates the idea of multi-issue negotiation and logrolling (cf.Fisher and Ury 1981,Malhotra and Bazerman 2008).8These two issues are represented by discrete and bounded setsXandY. Elements of these sets are calledalternatives, and each alternative represents a settlement for that issue. Each negotiator has a commonly known ranking over the alternatives, and these rankings are diametrically opposed.9In a bilateral trade problem, for example, the main issue may represent the quality of the product that the buyer is willing to purchase, whereas the supplementary issue may represent the price of the product. In an asset division problem, the main issue may represent the shares from major assets, whereas the supplementary issue may represent shares from minor assets.10 We also assume that some alternatives in the main issue may benonnegotiable(ordeal-breaker) for negotiators, and the set of deal-breaker alternatives is each negotiator’s private information. Thus, a negotiator’s private bargaining range determines her type. Depending on type realizations, private bargaining ranges may not overlap, and soa zone of mutual gainneed not always exist. We do not assert that negotiators must possess deal-breaker alternatives in every negotiation framework. Rather, we propose incorporating this potential into the traditional negotiation problem by avoiding the strict requirement that every alternative and potential outcome must always be deemed acceptable to the negotiators. Introducing this feature is an important extension of the standard model because ODR platforms, in practice, take this uncertainty seriously in their designs, although they ignore its impacts on parties’ incentive constraints. We are agnostic about why deal-breaker alternatives exist if or when they exist. One reason could be that these alternatives are inherently inferior to the negotiators’ outside options (i.e., the status quo), the observation that was both empirically and theoretically well supported in Psychology and Economics (see, for example,Masatlioglu and Ok (2014)and the references therein).11To model this informational asymmetry, we introduce anoutside option, which is commonly referred to as the BATNA (Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement) in the field.12A negotiator who ranks alternatives in the main issue as inferior to her outside option declares them deal-breakers. Bundles including such alternatives are calledunacceptableand would lead to the failure of mediation. Therefore, the main issue hasuncertain gains from mediation. All alternatives in the supplementary issue, however, are negotiable for all types of negotiators, and so the supplementary issue hascertain gains from mediation. In keeping with the practice of ODR, we assume that a (mediation) mechanism maps the negotiators’ private bargaining ranges in the main issue to a recommendation, either a bundle involving an alternative for each issue or an impasse (i.e., the outside option). A strategy-proof mechanism makes the truthful declaration of one’s bargaining range a dominant strategy. An efficient mechanism never recommends a bundle that can be (Pareto) improved upon. An individually rational mechanism never offers an alternative that falls outside a negotiator’s declared bargaining range. Consistent with many ODR platforms, negotiators are not required to report their private preferences over the bundles. Rather, for the sake of robustness, we maintain the assumption that a negotiator’s utility function can beanystrict and monotonic function over the set of bundles. We then search for mechanisms under which, across all strict and monotonic preferences, both negotiators voluntarily participate and declare their bargaining ranges truthfully, and no negotiator can be made better off without making her counterpart worse off. Therefore, if the negotiators’ utility functions were public information, which is a common assumption in the mechanism design literature, but bargaining ranges are still private, then our mechanisms are still strategy-proof, ex-post efficient, and individually rational. Our first main result is a complete characterization of the class of strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational mechanisms (Theorem 1). These mechanisms operate through an exogenously specifiedprecedence order(i.e., a partial hierarchy) over the alternatives in the main issue, and make offers from a special set of bundles, the so-calledlogrolling bundles. As the precedence order varies, the characterized class of mechanisms span what we refer to as thefamily of logrolling mechanisms. A visual characterization of this family demonstrates that a mechanism belongs to the family if and only if its matrix representation can be partitioned into rectangular regions (Theorem 3). The visual characterization simplifies the mechanics of the logrolling mechanisms and transforms them into easy-to-read diagrams that can be sequentially implemented as a menu of offers akin to those used by many online platforms such as eBay’s SquareTrade. This practical simplicity can make mediation more accessible and comprehensible. On the contrary, the algorithms of current ODR platforms are mostly unknown to users. Some platforms (e.g., Cybersettle) choose not to disclose their algorithms because of patent infringement concerns. Others (e.g., SmartSettle) also adopt a similar “black box” approach because their algorithms involve sophisticated and unintuitive integer optimization techniques. Designers of SmartSettle openly state that their multi-issue e-negotiation system cannot be used to its full potential by novices without the assistance of a facilitator (Lodder and Thiessen 2004).13This, however, may pose a serious concern from an economic design perspective because it is widely acknowledged in the literature that the simplicity and the transparency of the underlying mechanics of a mechanism are crucial for its efficacy.14 For our second main result, we explore the minimum amount of information the mediator needs to have about the negotiators’ underlying preferences (i.e., the necessary and sufficient conditions) to guarantee the existence of suitable mechanisms.Theorem 2is a complete characterization of the classes of preferences that admit strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational mechanisms. The necessary and sufficient condition is the so-calledquid pro quoproperty. This property imposes a form of substitutability between the main and supplementary issues.15It entails that issueYis rich enough so that a negotiator is able to make concessions in issueXto receive a more preferred alternative in issueY. The family of logrolling mechanisms nests interesting special members. When the precedence order is in line with the preference of a given negotiator over the logrolling bundles, we obtain the correspondingnegotiator-optimal mechanism. A negotiator-optimal mechanism represents situations when a mediator may be categorically biased toward one party in the dispute. Hence, we introduce a fairness criterion that is useful in judging the impartiality of the mediation processes. A central member of the family of strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational mediation mechanisms that satisfies this fairness criterion (Theorem 4) is the so-calledconstrained shortlistingmechanism. This mechanism recommends themedian logrolling bundlewhen it is mutually acceptable, and when it is not, favors the least-accepting negotiator. 1.1. Impossibility in Single-Issue Mediation and the Role of the Supplementary Issue It is easy to prove that there is no strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational mediation with a single issue, and the presence of the second issue is key for our possibility results. To make our point, we first consider the following simple, single-issue mediation problem. There is a single issue with two available alternatives,x1andx2. In a bilateral trade problem, for instance,x1may represent the high-quality product, whereasx2represents the low-quality product. Therefore, the set of all outcomes isB={x1,x2,ϕ}, whereϕrepresents no trade. It is public information that Negotiator 1 (the Buyer) prefers alternativex1tox2, and Negotiator 2 (the Seller) prefersx2tox1. The ranking of the outside option,ϕ, is each negotiator’s private information, and each negotiator ranks any nonnegotiable (or deal-breaker) alternative below the outside option. Each negotiator has only two types (i.e.,Ti={x1i,x2i}fori=1,2), and their preferences/rankings over the outcomes are as follows: A mechanismf:T1×T2→Bcan also be represented by the following matrix wherefℓ,j∈Bfor allℓ,j∈{1,2}: Individual rationality requiresf1,2=ϕsince there is no mutually acceptable alternative at this type profile. Efficiency and individual rationality implyfk,k=xkfork=1,2andf2,1∈{x1,x2}. Therefore, there are only two (deterministic) mechanisms satisfying individual rationality and efficiency in this simple framework. However, neither of these mechanisms is immune to strategic manipulation. To see this point, suppose thatf2,1=x1. In this case, typex12of Negotiator 2 would misreport her type when Negotiator 1 is of typex21because she guarantees her favorite outcomex2by reportingx22instead. Symmetrically, iff2,1=x2, then typex21of Negotiator 1 would lie about her type.16It is straightforward to extend this impossibility to the case with more than two alternatives. Next, consider the following framework with two issues,X={x1,x2}andY={y1,y2}, with the set of outcomesB={(x1,y1)(x1,y2),(x2,y1),(x2,y2),ϕ}. IssueYmay represent the set of feasible prices, where alternativesy1andy2represent low and high prices, respectively. It is also common knowledge that Negotiator 1 prefersy1toy2while Negotiator 2 prefersy2toy1, and both negotiators findy1andy2acceptable. Thus, issueYhascertain gains from mediation. As before, the types of each negotiator differ only by their rankings of the outside option in issueX, so each negotiator has two types. Now the mediation mechanism maps the negotiators’ private information to a bundle(x,y)inB. We make two assumptions regarding the negotiators’ preferences overB. First, they satisfy monotonicity: A bundle with a preferred alternative in each issue must be preferred. Second, preferences satisfy deal breaker property: A bundle that includes a deal-breaker alternative is ranked below the outside option,ϕ, whereas all other bundles are ranked above. Individual rationality of the mechanismfimplies thatf1,2=ϕsince, at this type profile, there is no mutually acceptable bundle. By the efficiency, individual rationality, and strategy-proofness off, we must havef1,1=(x1,y2)andf2,2=(x2,y1). To see this, considerf1,1. An efficient and individually rational mechanism, which respects the declared bargaining ranges, should suggest a bundle withx1at this entry because only those bundles are mutually acceptable at this type profile. Iff1,1is(x1,y1), then monotonicity and strategy-proofness would imply that bothf2,1andf2,2are also(x1,y1). However,f2,2corresponds to the type profile wherex1is declared nonnegotiable by Negotiator 2, so by the deal-breaker property,(x1,y1)is unacceptable for Negotiator 2, contradicting with individual rationality off. Therefore,f1,1must be either(x1,y2)orϕ. Monotonicity and efficiency eliminate the latter possibility. Thus,f1,1must be(x1,y2). Symmetric arguments suffice to show thatf2,2=(x2,y1). A similar logic implies thatf2,1must be either(x1,y2)or(x2,y1). We call these two bundleslogrolling bundlessince they pair a higher-ranked alternative in one issue with a (relatively) lower-ranked alternative in the other. A strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational mediation mechanismfwill always suggest a logrolling bundle whenever a mutually acceptable alternative exists. Clearly, these mechanisms are not strategy-proof at all preference domains that satisfy monotonicity and deal-breaker properties. Strategy-proofness requires that iff2,1=(x1,y2), then typex12of Negotiator 2 should prefer(x1,y2)over(x2,y1)so that she prefers to reveal her type truthfully, and iff2,1=(x2,y1), then typex21of Negotiator 1 should prefer(x2,y1)over(x1,y2)so that she prefers to reveal her type truthfully. This discipline on preferences is the essence of what we call thequid pro quoproperty, which turns out to be equivalent to a certain semilattice structure when there are more than two alternatives in each issue. Finally, although the supplementary issue is essential to incentivize the negotiators for truthful reporting, it does not have to be “more important” for the negotiators than the main issue. What matters for quid pro quo (formally defined inSection 3.2) is the comparison between the marginal gain and loss across bundles. To fix ideas, consider the following simple numerical example presented inTable 1.17Suppose that preferences over the bundles are additively separable and letu1(.)andv1(.)represent Negotiator 1’s preferences over the alternatives in issuesXandY, respectively. Therefore,U1(x,y)=u1(x)+v1(y)is Negotiator 1’s utility for bundle(x,y). Table 1.A Utility Function that Satisfies Quid Pro Quo Condition Table 1.A Utility Function that Satisfies Quid Pro Quo Condition The utility functionU1satisfies the quid pro quo condition, even though the worst alternative in issueXhas a significantly higher utility value than the best option in issueY. What is relevant for the quid pro quo is that the utility loss from switching to an inferior alternative in the main issue (which is 1 above) should be less than the utility gain from switching to a superior alternative in the supplementary issue (which is 3 above).
article.2. The Framework: The first part of this section presents the model and the terminology adopted throughout the paper. We defer our interpretation and discussion of key assumptions to the second part. 2.1. Preliminaries 2.1.1. Issues and Alternatives. Two negotiators, denoted by the setI={1,2}, are in a dispute over two issues. We refer to negotiators as “she” and to the mediator as “he”. We denote a generic negotiator byi∈Iand her opponent by−i∈I\{i}. LetX={x1,…,xm}andY={y1,…,yn}denote the finite set of orderedalternativesin themainand thesupplementaryissues, respectively, wheren≥m≥2. The supplementary issue becomes obsolete and irrelevant when the negotiators cannot agree on the main issue.18Depending on the type of negotiation, alternatives may represent prices, quantities, quality levels, shares of assets, possible delivery dates, employment positions, or various other contractual terms. It is public information that alternativexk(respectively,yk) fork∈Nis thekth best alternative for Negotiator 1 in the main issue (respectively, in the supplementary issue), and Negotiator 2 has diametrically opposed preferences in each individual issue19: Namely,xm(respectively,yn) is Negotiator 2’s best alternative,xm−1(respectively,yn−1) is her second-best alternative in the main issue (respectively, supplementary issue), and so on. We usex,x′, andy,y′to denote generic alternatives in issuesXandY, respectively, whenever it is not necessary to specify the rank of these alternatives. 2.1.2. Preferences and Types. We assume that not all alternatives in the main issue are up for negotiation and call such alternatives as “deal-breakers”. FollowingFisher and Ury (1981), we let the auxiliaryoutcomeϕrepresent a negotiator’s Best Alternative to A Negotiated Agreement (BATNA), which we refer as theoutside optionusing the convention in economics. An alternative in the main issue that is inferior toϕis adeal-breaker alternative. An alternative is anegotiable alternativeif it is not one of the deal-breaker alternatives. For a given negotiator, the set of alternatives that she finds negotiable determines her type. Letxkiwherek∈I={1,.…,m}, denote the type of Negotiatoriwhose least negotiable alternative isxk. That is, Negotiator 1 with typexk1ranksϕimmediately above alternativesxk+1throughxm, and Negotiator 2 with typexk2ranksϕimmediately above alternativesxk−1throughx1. LetTibe the set of all types of Negotiatori. Typexm1(respectively, typex12) is called the most accepting type of Negotiator 1 (respectively, of Negotiator 2) since she finds all alternatives negotiable. Each negotiator privately knows her true type, and we assume, without loss of generality, that all types find at least one alternative in the main issue negotiable. Therefore, each negotiator hasmmany types. More formally, letN(xk1)={xℓ∈X|ℓ≤k}andN(xk2)={xℓ∈X|ℓ≥k}denote the set of allnegotiable alternativesfor typexk1of Negotiator 1 and typexk2of Negotiator 2, respectively. Therefore,X\N(xki)denotes the set of alldeal-breaker alternativesfor typexkiof Negotiatori. We useti∈Tifor a generic type of Negotiatori. LetT=T1×T2denote the set of all type profiles. All alternatives in the supplementary issue are negotiable.20 The set of alloutcomesof the mediation, denoted byB, consists of the set of allbundlesand the outside optionϕ. Namely,B=(X×Y)∪{ϕ}.Acceptable bundlesof typetiof Negotiatoriis denoted byA(ti)={(x,y)∈X×Y|x∈N(ti)}. LetU1(respectively,U2) denote the set of allstrictanddecreasing(respectively,increasing) utility functions mapping all outcomes and private types into the reals.21We useui∈Uifor a generic utility of Negotiatori. Over the set of acceptable bundles, the same utility function represents the preferences of all types. Furthermore, all acceptable bundles are preferable to the outside option, and the outside option is preferable to all remaining (unacceptable) bundles. More formally, for alli∈I,ui∈Ui,ti,ti′∈Ti,b∈A(ti)∩A(ti′), and allb′∈B\A(ti)we haveui(b,ti)=ui(b,ti′)andui(b,ti)>ui(ϕ,ti)≥ui(b′,ti). The former equality is calledtype invariance, and the latter inequality is calleddeal-breaker property. We define the properties of a mechanism over a nonempty subsetDofU=U1×U2, which we call the set ofadmissible utility profiles. We letDito be the projection ofDoverUi.22Our interpretation is that Negotiatoriprivately knows her utility function and believes that her opponent’s true utility function is one of those inD−i. 2.1.3. Mechanisms. A direct (mediation) mechanismf:T→Brequires each negotiator to report her type (i.e., her negotiable alternatives), and either proposes a bundle(x,y), specifying an alternative for each issue or declares an impasse (i.e.,ϕ). For convenience, a mediation mechanismfis equivalently represented by anm×mmatrixf=[fk,ℓ](k,ℓ)∈I2wherefk,ℓ∈B. The rows (respectively, columns) of this matrix correspond to the types of Negotiator 1 (respectively, Negotiator 2). In particular, rowkdenotes typexk1of Negotiator 1, and columnkrefers to typexk2of Negotiator 2. Consider anyt=(xk1,xℓ2)∈T: Wheneverf(t)≠ϕ, we usefk,ℓXandfk,ℓYto denote the alternative that the mediation mechanismfchooses from issueXandY, respectively. Namely,f(t)=fk,ℓ=(fk,ℓX,fk,ℓY). Fix a set of admissible utility profilesD. Mechanismfisstrategy-proofoverDif for alli∈I,ti∈Ti, and allui∈Di, we haveui(f(ti,t−i),ti)≥ui(f(ti′,t−i),ti)for allti′∈Tiand allt−i∈T−i. Mechanismfis (ex post)individually rationaloverDif for alli∈I,t∈T, and allui∈Di, we haveui(f(t),ti)≥ui(ϕ,ti). Mechanismfis (ex post)efficientoverDif there exists not∈Tand nob∈Bsuch thatui(b,ti)≥ui(f(t),ti)for alli∈Iand allui∈Di, and the inequality is strict for at least oneiandui. We next introduce a series of definitions that will be key in presenting our main results. An injective functiony:X→Yisdecreasingif for allxk,xk′∈Xwithk>k′, there areyℓ,yℓ′∈Ywithℓ<ℓ′such thatyℓ=y(xk)andyℓ′=y(xk′). For any nonempty subsetSofXand a partial order⊵onX, letmaxS⊵denote the maximal element inSwith respect to⊵.23Namely, ifxS*=maxS⊵, thenxS*⊵xforallx∈S. Note that such a maximal element is not guaranteed to exist under an arbitrary (e.g., incomplete) partial order. Finally, the tuple(X,⊵)is called aposet(short for a partially ordered set) if⊵is a partial order onX. For a poset(X,⊵), we say that an elementx∈Xis anupper boundof a subsetS⊆Xwhenx⊵x′for allx′∈S. Theleast upper boundofSis the upper bound ofSthat is less than or equal to every upper bound ofS. Namely,xis a least upper bound ofSifx′⊵xfor all upper boundsx′ofS. Given a doubleton{x,x′}⊆X, let the join ofxandx′, denoted byx∨x′, be the least upper bound of the doubleton. A poset(X,⊵)is called ajoin semilatticeif every doubleton{x,x′}⊆Xhas a least upper bound inX. 2.2. Discussion In the mechanism design literature, agents’ utility functions and priors are often times assumed to be public information. This assumption is not harmful if the mechanisms are independent of these parameters. However, the well-known Wilson critique argues that good mechanisms in various settings depend heavily on the specific details of the agents’ utilities and priors, which undermines the practical application of these mechanisms. This argument is particularly alarming in the case of negotiations because noncommon priors are a common explanation for negotiation failures in the law and economics literature. Instead of Bayesian Nash equilibrium, we use the dominant strategy as our solution concept in this paper because it is widely accepted as a viable response to the Wilson critique. Our modeling assumptions are based on ODR practices, with the intention of designing robust mechanisms that are relevant to real-life applications. To achieve this, we assume two forms of asymmetric information in negotiations. The first is related to the negotiators’ bargaining ranges, which leads to private types. The second source of uncertainty is the agents’ preferences over (acceptable) bundles, which leads to the sets of admissible utility profiles. Each Negotiatoriprivately knows her type and preferences but assumes the opponent’s type is one of those inT−iand preferences is one of those inD−i. It is important to note that our model includes the standard case, where agents’ preferences over the acceptable bundles are common knowledge, as a special case. In line with common ODR practices, the mediator only solicits reports about the negotiators’ bargaining ranges and not their preferences. Consistent with our motivation, we search for mechanisms under which revealing their types truthfully is the agents’ best interest. Therefore, strategy-proofness requires truthful revelation of one’s type to be her dominant strategy, regardless of her underlying preferences. This is a stronger incentive requirement than a standard strategy-proofness property. The efficiency criterion is similarly modified in this framework. A mechanism is efficient if it never makes a Pareto-dominated proposal, and a proposal is Pareto-dominated if there is an alternative proposal that makes at least one agent better off without hurting the other, whatever their underlying preferences may be. We admit that, despite the improved strength of strategy-proofness, our efficiency definition implies a weaker form of efficiency than would be the case under a standard requirement. Although strategy-proofness and the ordinal approach can effectively remove the negative impacts of beliefs and cardinal utilities, they cannot completely eliminate the influence of preferences on finding a good mechanism. The well-known Gibbard and Satterthwaite impossibility result would apply if we were to assume that any utility is admissible. Therefore, it becomes necessary to restrict the domain of preferences in order to propose good mechanisms. Given that available alternatives in each issue are often limited and bounded in practice, the most intuitive domain restriction in a negotiation framework for us is what we call the deal-breaker property. Models of reference-dependent preferences are plausible explanations that are consistent with the deal-breaker property.24The outside option, or BATNA, as referred to in the negotiation literature, motivates the deal-breaker property. In the law and economics literature, BATNA often represents a negotiator’s subjective expected utility from the status quo outcome (e.g., an alternative resolution to the dispute, such as litigation). As such, in our modeling, a negotiator’s type embodies her prior claims and other extraneous private information that is not public information. Deal-breaker property is akin tounacceptabilityin compatibility-based preference settings in assignment problems, such as dichotomous preferences (e.g.,Bogomolnaia and Moulin 2004). Our preference formulation, however, is considerably less restrictive than dichotomous preferences because negotiators have the freedom to strictly prefer one negotiable alternative over another. It is worth noting that dealbreaker alternatives may also arise naturally (without a reference to an outside option) in a number of negotiation problems: In many business-to-business disputes, prior commitments, reputational concerns, or industrial practices render certain options and issues nonnegotiable. Similarly, in many international, interracial, or interreligious conflicts, some issues/solutions may be viewed as deal-breakers due to existing norms and values. We model mediation for disputes with two issues and finite sets of alternatives, but our results easily extend to cases with more than two issues or with a continuum of alternatives. The assumption that preferences over alternatives in each individual issue are diametrically opposed is without loss of generality. Under the requirement of efficiency, any dispute where preferences over alternatives are not diametrically opposed can be equivalently represented by a “reduced dispute” where “reduced preferences” are diametrically opposed. See the Online Appendix for further discussion. Mediation would potentially be a complicated multistage game between the negotiators and the mediator. The mediation protocol, whatever the details may be, produces proposals for agreement that are always subject to unanimous approval by the negotiators. That is, before finalizing the protocol, each negotiator has the right to veto the proposal and exercise her outside option. A version of the revelation principle guarantees that we can stipulate the following type of a two-stagedirect mechanism with veto rightswithout loss of generality when representing voluntary mediation. The two stages of the protocol are called theannouncementstage and theratificationstage. The announcement stage is characterized by a mechanismf:T→B. After being informed of her type, each Negotiatoriprivately reports a typeti∈Tito the mediator, who then proposes a bundlef(t1,t2)∈B. In the ratification stage, each party simultaneously and independently decides whether to accept or veto the proposed bundle. If both negotiators accept the proposed bundle, then it becomes the final outcome. If either or both negotiators veto the proposal, then mediation fails, and both parties get the outside option (i.e.,ϕ). We seek direct mechanisms with veto rights in which truthful reporting of types at the announcement stage is adominant strategy equilibriumand the mediator’s proposals are never vetoed in equilibrium. It immediately follows from the definitions that such an equilibrium exists if and only if mechanismfis strategy-proof and individually rational. To illustrate the compatibility of our setup with standard utility functions, we provide an example of a dispute in which issueXrepresents a major asset and issueYrepresents a minor asset. We later build on this example to illustrate our main results. Suppose that the negotiators need to divide positive surplusesx¯andy¯in issuesXandY, respectively. Therefore, we letX={x1,…,xm}andY={y1,…,yn}be two subsets of the real numbers, where an alternativex∈Xdenotes Negotiator 1’s share of the major asset andy∈Ythat of the minor asset. Negotiator 2 receives the complementary shares. Suppose0<xm<⋯<x1<x¯and0<yn<⋯<y1<y¯. The following two utility specificationsui:B→R+andvi:B→R+satisfy all the key properties we imposed earlier: Quasi-linear: Cobb-Douglas: The utility values for the outside option are normalized so thatui(ϕ)=vi(ϕ)=0for alli∈I. In this dispute, each negotiator prefers a larger share of any asset (i.e., preferences are diametrically opposed in both issues). When restricted to acceptable bundles,u1andu2are standard quasi-linear utility functions, andv1andv2are classic Cobb-Douglas utility functions.
article.3. Main Results: 3.1. Strategy-Proof Mediation We start with the characterization of the necessary conditions for a strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational mechanism. Given a type profile(xℓ1,xj2)∈Twithj≤ℓ, both negotiators have something to gain from mediation relative to an impasse. Therefore, we call the nonempty subset{xk∈X|j≤k≤ℓ}≡[xj,xℓ]ofXas thezone of mutual gain. Suppose that f is a strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational mechanism over a nonempty setD⊆U.Then there exists an injective and decreasing functiony:X→Y,and a partial (precedence) order⊵onXsuch that for anyt=(xℓ1,xj2)∈Twherej,ℓ∈I,ifj>ℓ,then the mechanism f recommends the outside optionϕ,and otherwise (i.e., ifj≤ℓ) the mechanism f recommends an alternative from the zone of mutual gain{xj,…,xℓ}that has the highest precedence according to⊵,denoted byx[xj,xℓ]*,and the alternative from the supplementary issue that corresponds toy(x[xj,xℓ]*).More formally, We defer the proof of all our results to the Online Appendix.Theorem 1states that when negotiators have a nonempty mutual zone of gain in the main issue, a desired mechanism first maximizes an exogenous partial order⊵to determine the chosen alternative for the main issue and then pairs this alternative with a corresponding alternative in the supplementary issue based on the decreasing functiony. This means that the mechanism must always make selections from a special set of bundles. At these bundles, a more preferred alternative from the main issue must be paired with a less preferred alternative from the supplementary issue (sinceyis decreasing). We interpret these bundles as representing possible “compromises” between the two issues. As such, we henceforth call these bundleslogrolling bundles. For a given decreasing functiony, letBybe the set of all logrolling bundles. When the main and supplementary issues have the same number of alternatives, this set is unique andy(xk)=yn−k+1. Otherwise (i.e., whenn>m), there can be multiple suchy’s, hence multiple classes of mechanisms. The setByof logrolling bundles constitutes the “backbone” of every strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational mechanism in the sense that the diagonal of any such mechanism (i.e., whenℓ=j, so there is a unique alternative in the zone of mutual gain) must always be comprised of logrolling bundles. The mediator has discretion over the choice of theprecedence order⊵onX. When the zone of mutual gain has more than one alternative (i.e.,ℓ>j), the logrolling bundle is selected according to the chosen precedence order⊵. If the zone of mutual gain is empty (i.e., whenℓ<j), then the mechanism always chooses the designated outside optionϕ. For the rest of the paper, we refer tofas alogrolling mechanismif it satisfies the properties described inTheorem 1and denote it byf⊵. The choice of the set of logrolling bundles, together with the precedence order, characterizes each mechanism. We will now present an example that illustrates these mechanisms. (A Logrolling Mechanism). Suppose the main issueXconsists of five alternatives (i.e.,m=5) and the supplementary issueYhas at least five alternatives. A set of logrolling bundles is thenBy={(x1,y(x1)),(x2,y(x2)),(x3,y(x3)),(x4,y(x4)),(x5,y(x5))}for some injective and decreasing functiony:X→Y. For notational simplicity, we letbk=(xk,y(xk))for allk=1,…,5. Let us construct the logrolling mechanismf⊵associated with the precedence order⊵, wherex5⊵x1⊵x4⊵x2⊵x3.Suppose we would like to determinef3,1⊵. The zone of mutual gain is[x1,x3]={x1,x2,x3}. The highest precedence alternative in this set isx1. Thus,f3,1⊵=b1. Similarly, to determinef4,2⊵we maximize⊵on[x2,x4]={x2,x3,x4}, which yieldsx4. Hence,f4,2⊵=b4. Alternatively, we can start from the diagonal and let the logrolling bundles “spread” in the southwestern direction following⊵. The main diagonal is filled with the members of the set of logrolling bundles,By. Namely, we havef1,1⊵=b1in the first diagonal entry,f2,2⊵=b2in the second diagonal entry, and so on. Since alternativex5has the highest precedence order, the corresponding logrolling bundle,b5, claims all the entries to its southwest, which amounts to the set of all entries on the bottom row to the left off5,5⊵. The second-highest precedence belongs tox1, and the corresponding logrolling bundle,b1, claims all the unfilled entries to its southwest. Thus, starting from the entryf1,1⊵on the main diagonal, all the remaining empty entries on the first column fill up withb1. Finally, whenever the negotiators have no mutually negotiable alternative in the main issue, the mechanism offersϕ. The following matrix inFigure 1shows this logrolling mechanism. 3.2. Full Characterization and Quid Pro Quo Theorem 1provides the necessary conditions that a strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational mediation mechanism must satisfy. However, a logrolling mechanism is not guaranteed to be strategy-proof in general. Therefore, we now search for a condition on preferences that guarantees strategy-proofness. Since the class of logrolling mechanisms contains the only candidates that can achieve the properties inTheorem 1, ensuring that a logrolling mechanism is strategy-proof automatically entails imposing discipline on preference profiles regarding how negotiators rank the logrolling bundles. To this end, we define a key notion. A nonempty set of admissible utility profiles,D⊆U, satisfiesquid pro quoif there exists a one-to-one and decreasing functiony:X→Yand a partial order⪰yoverX, satisfying the following: For any two distinct alternatives of the main issuex,x′∈X, we havex⪰yx′if there exists a negotiator,i∈I, such that (i) she ranks alternativex′above x, and ranks bundle(x,y(x))above bundle(x′,y(x′))whenever she finds both alternatives negotiable, that is, for allti∈Tiwithx,x′∈N(ti)and allui∈Di,ui(x′,y(x),ti)≥ui(x,y(x),ti)≥ui(x′,y(x′),ti), and (ii) there is no alternative in the supplementary issue,y∈Y, that she ranks abovey(x′)and belowy(x)such that bundle(x′,y)Pareto dominates bundle(x,y(x)), that is, there exists noy∈Ysatisfyinguj(x′,y,tj)≥uj(x,y(x),tj)for allj∈I, alluj∈Dj, and alltj∈Tjwithx,x′∈N(tj), where the inequality is strict for at least one such j,ujandtj. For any nonempty zone of mutual gainS⊆X, the poset(S,⪰y)is a join semilattice. When the negotiators’ utility functions satisfy quid pro quo, we letΠDdenote the set of all partial orders induced by the set of admissible utility profiles inD. Namely,ΠDis the set of all partial orders⪰yoverXsuch that the decreasing functionyand⪰ysatisfyDefinition 1. Quid pro quo implies that negotiators are willing to make concessions in the main issue for more favorable treatment in the supplementary issue. Put differently, it should be possible to find some alternatives in issueYthat are sufficiently attractive for at least one of the negotiators to reverse her ranking of some alternatives in the main issue when they are bundled together. Specifically, condition(1.i)says that for some pairs of negotiable alternativesx,x′in the main issue, there must be a negotiator such that although she prefersx′tox(the first inequality in(i)), there is a pair of alternativesy,y′, wherey=y(x)andy′=y(x′), in the supplementary issue with the property that she prefers(x,y)to(x′,y′)(the second inequality in(i)). Such possibility of a preference reversal induces the partial orderx⪰yx′. Condition(1.ii)ensures that all logrolling bundles generated byyare Pareto efficient. This condition is satisfied when mediation is described as a division of surplus problem (seeExample 1) and negotiators have standard utility functions (seeExample 3). These preference reversals define a partial order onX, and condition(2)requires that this partial order, together with any zone of mutual gain, form a join semilattice. We are now ready to provide a full characterization result. There exists a mechanism f satisfying strategy-proofness, efficiency, and individual rationality over a nonempty setD⊆Uif and only ifDsatisfies quid pro quo and there is a partial order⪰y∈ΠDsuch thatf=f⪰y. Theorem 2states that quid pro quo is both necessary and sufficient for the existence of strategy-proof, efficient, and individual rational mediation mechanisms, and any such mechanism must be a logrolling mechanism associated with a precedence order⪰yinduced by the domain of admissible utility profilesD. It is useful to discuss the role quid pro quo plays in achieving strategy-proofness. Intuitively, a negotiator may change the outcome and profitably game a mechanism by eitherunder-reportingher bargaining range (i.e., declaring some negotiable alternatives as deal-breaker) orover-reporting(i.e., declaring some deal-breaker alternatives as negotiable). If an alternative in the main issue is a deal-breaker, then falsely declaring it negotiable under a logrolling mechanism can never improve the outcome by the deal-breaker property of preferences. Indeed, if a negotiator over-reports, this expands the zone of mutual gain. However, since the chosen bundle is based on a (fixed) precedence order, the negotiator either gets the same outcome as when she is truthful or gets a bundle with a deal-breaker alternative. On the other hand, falsely declaring a negotiable alternative as a deal-breaker by under-reporting may result in a better alternative for the main issue. However, it will now be bundled with a worse alternative from the supplementary issue under a logrolling mechanism. By the quid pro quo property, this bundle is always inferior to the original bundle under truthful reporting. An important takeaway ofTheorem 2is that not all multi-issue disputes admit good mechanisms, and in this sense, not all cases aresolvable, despite the best efforts of the designers. It all boils down to the negotiators’ underlying interests and substitutability of the issues. Consider, for example, a scenario where alternatives of issueYhave little appeal for the negotiators compared with those in issueX(e.g., preferences are lexicographic over the two issues). Then there is little reason to suspect that the impossibility in the single-issue case (see Section 1.2) will be overturned in the two-issue world. In fact, quid pro quo property fails to hold in such preference domains. Quid pro quo constitutes the limits of solvable disputes. If we map our discrete model into a classic exchange economy, where alternatives represent quantities of goods, then well-known utility functions, such as a CES or a quasi-linear utility, satisfy quid pro quo (seeExamples 1and3). (Quid Pro Quo Under Standard Preferences). We reconsider the utility functionsu1andu2inExample 1and discuss conditions that guarantee the quid pro quo property, and hence strategy-proofness. For concreteness, consider the following simple numerical example: Suppose thatx¯=10,y¯=6,X={1,3,5,7,9}andy(x)=(11−x)/2, so that the set of logrolling bundles isBy={(1,5),(3,4),(5,3),(7,2),(9,1)}. Given the utility functionsu1andu2inExample 1, quid pro quo is satisfied whenαi∈(0,3/5]for eachi∈I. Indeed, while Negotiator 1 prefers higher values ofx, she prefers(x,y(x))over(x′,y(x′))for allx,x′∈Xwithx′>x. Based solely on 1’s preferences, this induces a complete order onXwherex⪰y1x′whenx′>x. Similarly, Negotiator 2’s preferences over bundles satisfy an analogous reversal. Based solely on 2’s preferences, this induces a complete order onXwherex⪰y2x′whenx>x′. Consequently, any linear order onXsatisfies Condition(1.i)ofDefinition 1. Indeed, any logrolling mechanism associated withByand any well-defined partial order onXis strategy-proof under these preferences.25 3.3. A Visual Characterization of the Class of Logrolling Mechanisms To provide further insight into the logrolling mechanisms that are characterized byTheorems 1and2, we offer a geometric analysis of these mechanisms. We first take a mechanismf=[fℓ,j](ℓ,j)∈I2and introduce a couple of definitions to represent different rectangular and triangular regions of this matrix. In the following two definitions, we slightly abuse notation and terminology in order to keep track of the entries contained in a rectangular/triangular region. Namely, we usefℓ,jto refer to entry(ℓ,j)of the matrix rather than the specific bundle that mechanismfassigns to that entry. Consider the entryfk,kfor somek∈Iand an entry that lies (weakly) to its southwest,fℓ,jwith1≤j≤k≤ℓ≤m. Therectangleinduced byfk,kandfℓ,j, denoted by□ℓ,jk, is the set of all entries in the rectangular region of the matrix (inclusively) enveloped between rows k andℓand columns k and j. Namely,□ℓ,jk=∪j≤s≤kk≤t≤ℓ{ft,s}. Thetriangleinduced by an entryfℓ,jwith1≤j≤ℓ≤m, denoted by△ℓ,j,is the set of all entries in the triangular region of the matrix that is (inclusively) enveloped by the entryfℓ,j, rowℓ, column j, and the main diagonal. Namely,△ℓ,j=∪j≤k≤ℓ{fk,j,fk,j+1,…,fk,k}. A rectangle/triangle is merely a collection of entries of the matrix induced by mechanismf(i.e., sets of pairs of indexes). Note that an entry on the main diagonal is a special triangle (and also a special rectangle) that consists of a singleton entry. Furthermore, the entire main diagonal of the matrix and all the entries to its southwest constitute the largest possible triangle△m,1.Given a triangle△ℓ,j,its entries that lie on the main diagonal are said to be on thehypotenuseof△ℓ,j. A partition of the lower half of the matrix is called arectangular (triangular) partitionif and only if it is the union of disjoint rectangles (triangles).26 (Visual Characterization).Lety:X→Ybe an injective and decreasing function and⪰ybe a partial order onX.The following statements are equivalent. (i)f is equivalent to a logrolling mechanism, namelyf(t)=f⪰y(t),at all type profilest∈Twhere the zone of mutual gain is nonempty. (ii)The triangle△m,1has a rectangular partition such that f assigns a unique bundle from the set of logrolling bundlesByto each rectangle in this partition.27 Part(ii)ofTheorem 3states that a logrolling mechanismfcan be represented as the union ofmdisjoint rectangular regions. Each rectangle has a distinct corner entry on the main diagonal that contains the logrolling bundle that fills up the entire rectangle. Procedurally, these rectangles are obtained as follows. Given the precedence order⪰yonX, start with the logrolling bundle with the highest-precedence alternative (i.e., highest-precedence bundle). Starting from the entry of this bundle on the hypotenuse of the largest triangle,△m,1, let it fill up all the entries located to its southwest. This creates the first and largest rectangle□, and leads to a triangular partition of△m,1\□. Next, pick any triangle from this partition and let the highest-precedence bundle on the hypotenuse of this triangle fill up all the entries that are located to its southwest. This leads to a second rectangle□′as well as a unique triangular partition of△m,1\{□,□′}. The process can be iterated in this fashion until the entire triangle△m,1is partitioned intomdisjoint rectangles inmsteps.Figure 2(a)provides an illustration of one such partitioning, wherebk=(xk,y(xk))fork=1,…,9. This process effectively traces the semilattice(X,⪰y)inFigure 2(b). Conversely, any such geometric set, namely any rectangular partition of△m,1, can be used to construct a precedence order and a corresponding logrolling mechanism. 3.4. A Practical Formulation of Logrolling Mechanisms Dispute resolution protocols of several ODR platforms, such as SquareTrade, operate by generating a menu of offers from which negotiators are asked to make selections sequentially. In light ofTheorem 3, the working principles of the logrolling mechanisms lead to a similar interpretation that is also reminiscent of the divide-and-choose mechanisms in fair division literature. In particular, a logrolling mechanism can be thought to work as a “shortlisting mechanism” in a decentralized fashion: One negotiator offers a shortlist of bundles as negotiable solutions for the dispute, the mediator communicates these options to the other negotiator who then chooses her favorite bundle from this list. To see this, observe that when Negotiator 1 reports her type asxℓ1, it can be viewed as Negotiator 1 forming a shortlist consisting of all the bundles on rowℓ. When faced with the list of bundles Negotiator 1 offers, Negotiator 2 indeed picks the bundlefℓ,jsince it is her favorite negotiable bundle on rowℓby strategy-proofness. If the roles of the negotiators in this procedure were reversed, then the outcome would still be the same by symmetric arguments.28 For a more specific example, consider the logrolling mechanism depicted inFigure 2(a). Suppose that Negotiator 1 is of typex31. Then we can think of her as proposing the shortlist{b2,b3,ϕ}to the other negotiator when she truthfully declares her type. The corresponding shortlists for other announcements asx51andx71are{b2,b4,b5,ϕ}and{b2,b6,b7,ϕ}, respectively. Under this interpretation, a logrolling mechanism specifies a set of shortlisted bundles that a negotiator can offer to the other party for each possible type she reports. By reporting a more accepting type, the proposer may add new bundles or remove some from her shortlist.Theorem 3implies that as negotiators declare more accepting types, suggested shortlists must satisfy some kind of regularity in the sense that a previously removed bundle can never be added back to the shortlist. For the logrolling mechanism depicted inFigure 2(a), for instance, if Negotiator 1 switches fromx31tox51, she adds bundlesb4andb5to the shortlist and removesb3. If she switches fromx51tox71, then she addsb6andb7and removesb4andb5from the shortlist. Note for this logrolling mechanism that once bundlesb3,b4orb5are removed, they are never added back in.
article.4. Special Members of the Logrolling Fam: We next visit interesting members of the logrolling family. At the outset, we assume that the preference domain is such that all members of the family are strategy-proof (e.g., negotiators have quasi-linear preferences). Specifically, we assume throughout this section that quid pro quo is satisfied in the following stronger sense: The domain of preferencesD⊂Uadmits an injective and decreasing functiony:X→Ysuch that for alli∈I,x,x′∈Xandti∈Tiwithx,x′∈N(ti),ifui(x′,y,ti)≥ui(x,y,ti)for somey∈Y,thenui(x,y(x),ti)≥ui(x′,y(x′),ti)for allui∈Di. Under this assumption, any partial order⪰yonXsatisfiesDefinition 1, so byTheorem 2, all members of the logrolling family (and only these mediation mechanisms) are strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational. Therefore,Assumption 1renders a more meaningful comparison of the members of the logrolling family. Three notable members are worth pointing out. Anegotiator-optimal mechanismrepresents a situation of extreme partiality to one side of the dispute and is constructed by using the precedence order implied by a negotiator’s preferences over the logrolling bundles. Specifically, the Negotiator 1-optimal mechanism takes⪰y1, wherexm⪰y1xm−1⪰y1⋯⪰y1x1, whereas the Negotiator 2-optimal mechanism takes⪰y2, wherex1⪰y2x2⪰y2⋯⪰y2xm. In case of severe disagreement (i.e., when the zone of mutual gain is empty), the outside option,ϕ, is the outcome. The two dual mechanisms are shown below inFigure 3, (a)and (b) for the case ofm=n=5. A negotiator-optimal mechanism always chooses the corresponding negotiator’s most preferred bundle among the acceptable logrolling bundles. The analogous shortlisting mechanism is rather simple: The favored negotiator’s shortlist includes only two bundles, which are her favorite acceptable logrolling bundle and the outside option.29Clearly, these two polar members of the family of logrolling mechanisms are highly unattractive in practice.30Fortunately, there is a remarkable member of this family who treats negotiators symmetrically. Impartiality entails focusing on a central element of the set of logrolling bundles as a compromise. It is then intuitive for the mediator to recommend amedianlogrolling bundle when it is mutually negotiable or seek a bundle as close to it as possible when it is not. Within the family of logrolling mechanisms, this is achieved simply by assigning the highest precedence to a median logrolling bundle, the next precedence to those bundles that are closest to the chosen median, and so on, and the lowest precedence to the extremal logrolling bundles. This motivates the following type of mechanism, which we call aconstrained shortlisting (CS) mechanism. Letk∈{k¯,k¯}be the index of a median alternative in the main issue, wherek¯=⌈(m+1)/2⌉andk¯=⌊(m+1)/2⌋. A mechanism is aconstrained shortlisting mechanism, denotedfCS=[fℓ,j](ℓ,j)∈I2, if it is a logrolling mechanism that is associated with a precedence order⪰yCS, wherexk⪰yCSxk−1⪰yCS⋯⪰yCSx1andxk⪰yCSxk+1⪰yCS⋯⪰yCSxm, andfℓ,jCS=ϕwheneverℓ<j. When the number of alternatives in the main issue is odd, there is a unique constrained shortlisting mechanism. When the number of alternatives is even, however, a constrained shortlisting mechanism prescribes two possible types of outcomes.31Figure 4illustrates the constrained shortlisting mechanism for the case ofm=n=5. When the number of alternatives is odd, the CS mechanism is a symmetric member of the logrolling mechanisms family.32In the lower half of the matrix, it acts as a negotiator-optimal mechanism whenever the median alternative in the main issue is not a member of the zone of mutual gain and recommends the median logrolling bundle whenever the zone of mutual gain includes the median alternative. In other words, when both negotiators find at least half of the alternatives in the main issue negotiable, the mechanism chooses the median logrolling bundle; and when one negotiator finds at least half of the alternatives negotiable while the other finds less than half of the alternatives negotiable, the mechanism chooses the less-accepting negotiator’s favorite acceptable logrolling bundle. In discrete resource allocation problems where agents are endowed with ordinal preference rankings, fairness properties (together with efficiency) have often proved difficult to attain in the absence of monetary transfers or a randomization device. It is nevertheless worthwhile to investigate whether it is possible for a member of the logrolling mechanisms family to achieve alternative fairness requirements beyond symmetry. We next formulate one such ordinal fairness notion as a normative requirement for our context. Given the negotiators’ preferences over alternatives (not including the outside option), letri(z)∈Idenote Negotiatori’s ranking of a negotiable alternativez∈Z∈{X,Y}. For normalization, we reassign ranks 1 throughmfor the chosen alternatives inY(i.e., those alternatives that are in the range ofy) and set the ranking of the outside option to be zero.33Given the logrolling mechanismf=[fℓ,j](ℓ,j)∈I2, therank variance of the bundlefℓ,jis defined as34 Then, therank varianceof a mechanismfis the total sum of the rank variance of all possible outcomes off, and defined as Intuitively, the larger the differences between the two negotiators’ rankings of the alternatives in a given bundle, the higher the rank variance of that bundle. For example, while never recommended by a logrolling mechanism, the bundles(x1,y1)and(xm,ym)have the highest rank variance. Despite making one negotiator as well off as possible, they make the opposite negotiator as worse off as possible. In this sense, the larger the rank variance of a mediation mechanism, the more skewed it is toward extremal bundles. A mediation mechanism minimizes rank variance within the class of logrolling mechanisms if and only if it is a constrained shortlisting mechanism.
article.5. Related Literature: Although mediation is a highly interdisciplinary topic, little attention has been given to the design of online dispute resolution mechanisms. The only works we are aware of are experimental studies that focus on market design aspects of feedback revision rules (cf.Bolton et al. 2018,2023). The law and economics literature on settlement negotiations under asymmetric information is extensive.35Our approach is fundamentally different from this literature, both conceptually and methodologically. In asettlement negotiation, communications between parties revolve around evidence, rule of law, and witnesses; and when negotiations fail, trial is generally the next step. By contrast, we study what is referred to asfacilitative mediationin which the goal is not to determine who is right or who has a stronger case but rather to explore mutually negotiable resolutions. In economic theory, negotiations are generally modeled through the lens of traditional bargaining models (e.g.,Nash 1953,Rubinstein 1982,Myerson and Satterthwaite 1983). However, existing bargaining models offer limited insights into the practicality of offering compromises in multidimensional deals; a wisdom often voiced by experts in the field (Fisher and Ury 1981,Malhotra and Bazerman 2008). A central question in bargaining under incomplete information is whether private information prevents the bargainers from reaping all possible gains from trade. The pioneering work ofMyerson and Satterthwaite (1983)gave a negative answer. In this literature, only a limited number of papers study the topic of mediation with outside options.36However, their focus is also on settlement negotiations. Departing from the mechanism design literature and in a similar spirit to us,Jackson et al. (2023)argue that “Existing bargaining models shed no light on [the] perceived wisdom [of practitioners] that offering multiple deals and searching for the right one is central to negotiations.” They also emphasize that mechanisms that are based on the utility functions or beliefs of the agents can be viewed as impractical (see alsoWilson 1987andSatterthwaite et al. 2014). The practical formulation of our log rolling mechanisms as shortlisting mechanisms with a menu of offers (Section 3.4) directly addresses their former critique, while our ordinal approach, together with dominant strategy implementation, addresses the latter. With some caveats, a dispute resolution problem can also be interpreted as a type of fair division problem involving indivisible items. Logrolling mechanisms allow one negotiator to effectively reduce the set of possible outcomes to a shortlist, from which the other negotiator makes her favorite selection. In that sense, logrolling mechanisms are reminiscent of the well-known biblical rule of divide-and-choose, which has been extensively studied in fair cake-cutting problems. Two advantages of a logrolling mechanism relative to divide-and-choose are that it is strategy-proof (whenever preferences satisfy quid pro quo), and its outcome is independent of the ordering of the negotiators. More generally, the fair division literature almost exclusively focuses on fairness and efficiency issues due to inherent incompatibilities with strategy-proofness similar to those in the multiunit assignment context; seeBrams and Taylor (1996)for an excellent overview. When outside options do not exist, our setting roughly resembles a type of multiunit assignment problem (e.g., course allocation) in which only certain assignments are feasible. Some of the existing point allocation-based ODR mechanisms, for example, SmartSettle, are akin to course-bidding mechanisms that have been discussed in this literature. Both theoretical and experimental research have shown that such auction mechanisms can perform rather poorly due to the perverse incentives they generate.37A major insight from that context immediately carries over to dispute resolution: When a bidding mechanism is used, a disputant can find it strategically advantageous not to “waste” points on less-contested issues despite having a truly high valuation of these issues. This, in turn, translates into strategic reports that are not representative of true preferences. Nonetheless, the multiunit assignment setting provides little reason to remain optimistic for positive results. The main result of this literature is that the only strategy-proof and efficient mechanisms are serial dictatorships; for example, seePápai (2001),Klaus and Miyagawa (2002), andEhlers and Klaus (2003). Clearly, dictatorship mechanisms have little appeal in a dispute resolution situation. A novelty of our approach that distinguishes it from the literature on fair division and assignment is that we do not ask agents to report their preferences. Instead, we maintain the view that a dispute is solvable so long as the underlying preferences allow for it. This not only frees us from further complications related to the choice of a suitable preference reporting language but is also consistent with many of the ODR practices and recommendation systems used in e-commerce. Finally, with the hope of arriving at possibility results, there is a tradition of searching for strategy-proof mechanisms in restricted economic environments that make it possible to escape the famous Arrow-Gibbard-Satterthwaite impossibility results.38We also add to this literature by introducing and characterizing an entirely new class of strategy-proof and efficient mechanisms.
article.6. Conclusion: Mediation is the preferred alternative dispute resolution method thanks to the cost-effectiveness, speed, and convenience it affords to all parties involved. The need for structured and rigorous mediation protocols in practice has often been stressed by researchers and practitioners alike. Online dispute resolution platforms are often based on automation and rely on mechanized negotiation protocols. However, existing dispute resolution protocols do not account for the incentives faced by disputants. Taking a foundational mechanism design approach to this problem, we sought systematic mechanisms for delivering consistent, transparent, and objective recommendations while giving strong incentives to the disputants to be truthful. We considered mechanisms that have a simple preference reporting language; negotiators only report their bargaining ranges (i.e., least negotiable alternatives) in the main issue. It turns out that complementing the main issue with a second one—a piece of advice often raised by pioneers in the field—is key to achieving strategy-proof, efficient, and individually rational mechanisms. Any such mechanism belongs to the family of logrolling mechanisms, which require that the mediator’s recommendation always be a logrolling bundle (a bundle that complements a more preferred alternative in one issue with a less preferred alternative in the other) when a mutual agreement is feasible. A sufficient and necessary condition for strategy-proofness is the quid pro quo property of preferences that necessitates the alternatives in the second issue to be interesting enough to allow for preference reversals. The constrained shortlisting mechanism is the central member within the characterized class and makes recommendations as close to the median logrolling bundle as possible. Protocols vulnerable to gaming can result in systematically unfair and inefficient outcomes or may fail to solve otherwise resolvable disputes. As such, failing to take parties’ incentives into account can severely reduce the purported benefits of online dispute resolution systems. Our theoretical analysis may help shed light on the fundamental forces at work when efficiency is sought together with robust incentives. An interesting open question is how to incorporate full preference elicitation from negotiators into the mechanism design problem. Further research is needed on this front since allowing negotiator types to also include preferences would readily give rise to a more sophisticated preference reporting language than ours as well as new incentive and welfare considerations. Although we leave this direction for future investigation, we contend that the class of mechanisms characterized here would constitute an ideal starting point for developing more sophisticated dispute resolution protocols.
